section.title: AI and work
section.intro: How AI changes professional work – which tasks get easier, which get harder, and what organizations should do about it.

resources.title: Management as AI Superpower
resources.author: Ethan Mollick
resources.description: Argues that working with AI agents requires management skills, not prompt engineering. Delegating, reviewing output, providing context, catching errors – the skills of a good manager.

resources.title: Why AI at Work Makes Us So Anxious
resources.author: Harvard Business Review
resources.description: Why the pace of AI change creates genuine distress even among people technically capable of adapting.

resources.title: On Holding Back the Strange Grief of AI
resources.author: Ethan Mollick
resources.description: On the experience of watching a machine do well at something a person has spent years getting good at.

resources.title: Why Gen AI Feels So Threatening to Workers
resources.author: Harvard Business Review
resources.description: Identifies three threats beyond job loss: competence (AI approximates expert output), autonomy (decisions shift to systems workers don't control), and belonging (team identity shifts when core tasks change).

resources.title: AI Doesn't Reduce Work – It Intensifies It
resources.author: Harvard Business Review
resources.description: AI raised the baseline of expected output. Instead of fewer hours, more deliverables at higher velocity, without corresponding changes to expectations or staffing.

resources.title: How Do Workers Develop Good Judgment in the AI Era?
resources.author: Harvard Business Review
resources.description: Senior professionals can evaluate AI output; junior staff can't yet. How do people build expertise when AI handles the tasks that develop judgment?

resources.title: The Anthropic Economic Index
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.description: Analysis of how Claude is actually used. Finds a net deskilling pattern: AI covers higher-education tasks first, not manual labor. Workers with advanced degrees doing knowledge work are most affected.

resources.title: Co-Intelligence: Living and Working with AI
resources.author: Ethan Mollick
resources.description: Working alongside AI – creativity, education, and organizational change.

resources.title: Centaurs and Cyborgs on the Jagged Frontier
resources.author: Ethan Mollick
resources.description: Based on a BCG study: AI makes some tasks dramatically easier and others worse. The "jagged frontier" is the uneven boundary between what AI does well and what it doesn't.

resources.title: Artificial Intelligence and the Health Workforce
resources.author: OECD
resources.description: AI's impact on health sector employment across OECD countries. Skill shifts, training gaps, and where workforce readiness falls short.

resources.title: Navigating the Jagged Technological Frontier (Study)
resources.author: Dell'Acqua et al.
resources.description: The research behind the "jagged frontier" concept. BCG consultants using GPT-4 were 25% more productive on tasks inside the frontier and produced lower-quality work on tasks outside it.

resources.title: Gen-AI: AI and the Future of Work
resources.author: International Monetary Fund
resources.description: Global perspective on AI and employment, including differential impacts across income levels and countries.

resources.title: Artificial Intelligence and the Politics of Avoidance in Global Health
resources.author: Leah Shipton & Lucia Vitale
resources.description: Argues AI is the latest technology-based solution that avoids addressing fundamental determinants of health inequities.
